Enhancing the sensitivity and accuracy of wearable glucose biosensors: A systematic review on the prospects of mutarotase - ScienceDirect Enhancing the sensitivity and accuracy of wearable glucose biosensors: A systematic review on the prospects of mutarotase Wearable glucose biosensors (WGBs) face significant challenges due to pH, temperature, and skin pollutants affecting glucose detection accuracy by disrupting D-glucose anomeric equilibrium. Updating the present level of research on the use of MUT in glucose sensors is critical for furthering the development of wearable glucose biosensors (WGB) designed for alternate bodily fluids such as sweat. This systematic review evaluates the advancements and prospects of mutarotase (MUT) in enhancing the sensitivity and accuracy of wearable glucose biosensors (WGBs).

Enhancing the sensitivity and accuracy of wearable glucose biosensors: A systematic review on the prospects of mutarotase

Highlights
----------

*   •The systematic review examined the role of mutarotase in glucose detection. 
*   •Importance of addressing glucose anomeric properties for accurate monitoring. 
*   •Challenges and opportunities in integrating mutarotase into current glucose biosensors. 

Abstract
--------

Wearable glucose biosensors (WGBs) face significant challenges due to pH, temperature, and skin pollutants affecting glucose detection accuracy by disrupting D-glucose anomeric equilibrium. Although mutarotase (MUT) has historically addressed these challenges, recent research attention on MUT is limited. This systematic review evaluates the performance of biosensors utilizing MUT for enhanced glucose detection. Comprehensive searches across PubMed, Scopus, and Web of Science identified 1,603 studies, of which 13 met PRISMA standards and were selected. Data were extracted and synthesized using pre-designed forms, with results presented through charts and tables. The reviewed studies did not provide clear data about the influence of MUT on the limit of detection (LOD). However, glucose biosensors incorporating MUT demonstrated sensitivity across a broad linear detection range, potentially eliminating the need for sample dilution in some instances. MUT also ensures a more accurate representation of total glucose levels in a sample, achieving complete glucose recovery (100 %) in 6 s in amperometric experiments and within 3-4 min in colorimetric, luminometric, polarimetric, and spectrophotometric studies. Despite stability concerns in 8 % of the studies, MUT proved effective across various pH (5.0–8.5) and temperature (20—37 ∘C) ranges. These results highlight the potential of MUT in advancing glucose wearable biosensing technology.

1. Introduction
---------------

Glucose biosensors have revolutionized diabetes management [[1]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0001), [[2]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0002), [[3]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0003), [[4]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0004), [[5]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0005), historically relying on invasive techniques like finger-prick blood sampling [[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0006), [[7]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0007). Recent advancements have shifted focus towards non-invasive technologies, particularly wearable glucose biosensors (WGB), aimed at improving user experience and accuracy in glucose monitoring [[8]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0008), [[9]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0009), [[10]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0010), [[11]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0011). However, the widespread adoption of wearable biosensors faces formidable challenges. Environmental variability, including changes in pH, temperature, and humidity, poses significant obstacles to sensor accuracy and reliability [[12]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0012), [[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0013), [[14]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0014), [[15]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0015). Overcoming these challenges requires robust sensor designs that can mitigate the effects of environmental fluctuations, ensuring consistent and precise measurements over time. Additionally, biofouling where biological substances accumulate on sensor surfaces presents a persistent issue that can degrade sensor performance, necessitating advanced materials and coatings to maintain sensor efficacy during prolonged use [[16]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0016), [[17]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0017).

Biocompatibility is another critical concern, ensuring that wearable sensors are hypoallergenic and non-irritating for diverse user populations [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0018), [[19]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0019), [[20]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0020). Establishing reliable correlations between biomarkers measured in alternative body fluids (such as sweat or interstitial fluid) and blood levels is essential but requires rigorous validation across different demographics and health conditions. Power management emerges as a fundamental challenge, as wearable devices must balance energy efficiency with the need for sustained, uninterrupted monitoring [[21]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0021), [[22]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0022), [[23]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0023). This necessitates innovative approaches to energy harvesting or storage to extend operational lifetimes and reduce reliance on frequent battery changes, thereby enhancing user convenience and compliance.

User acceptance and usability are pivotal for long-term adoption [[24]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0024). Ensuring ergonomic designs that integrate seamlessly into daily life, coupled with lightweight, unobtrusive form factors, enhances user comfort and willingness to use these devices consistently. Moreover, navigating complex regulatory pathways and demonstrating adherence to stringent standards for accuracy, reliability, and safety adds substantial complexity and cost to sensor development and commercialization. Finally, achieving cost-effective manufacturing and scalability is crucial for broad accessibility, alongside improving sensor durability to withstand real-world conditions over extended periods [[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0013), [[25]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0025). Addressing these multifaceted challenges through interdisciplinary collaboration and continuous innovation is paramount to realizing the full potential of wearable biosensors in personalized healthcare, ultimately improving patient outcomes worldwide.

Wearable Biosensors operate through a stepwise detection process that involves bio-recognition, transduction, and signal processing ([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#fig0001)) [[26]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0026), [[27]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0027), [[28]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0028). Initially, the bio-recognition element specifically interacts with the target analyte, such as an enzyme, antibody, or aptamer, generating an initial signal. This signal is then converted into a measurable form during the transduction step, which can utilize electrochemical, optical, or piezoelectric methods. The final step involves signal processing, where the transduced signal is amplified, filtered, and converted into a readable format, ultimately providing meaningful data to the user. In the development of these sensors, the first two detection steps, bio-recognition and transduction, receive most research attention. Researchers focus on enhancing the specificity and sensitivity of the bio-recognition elements, as well as improving the efficiency and accuracy of the transduction mechanisms.

![Image 12: Fig. 1](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S266605392400047X-gr1.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (317KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S266605392400047X-gr1_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (317KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S266605392400047X-gr1.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 1. General mechanism of glucose detection using enzymatic biosensors in the presence of mutarotase: Initially, the bio-recognition element (glucose oxidase enhanced with mutarotase) interacts with glucose, generating hydrogen peroxide **(Step 1)**. This signal is converted during the transduction step via (3) colorimetric/optical methods (oxidation of colored or light-emitting substrate) or (4) electrochemical methods (electron transfer) **(Step 2)**. The final step processes the signal by amplifying, filtering, and converting it into a readable format, providing meaningful data to the user **(Step 3)**. (Note: Reaction (3) may require peroxidase enzyme, depending on the substrate’s nature).

Depending on the transduction method, biosensors could generally be classified into electrochemical, optical or Piezoelectric [[29]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0029), [[30]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0030), [[31]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0031), [[32]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0032). While the transduction step is also critical, the effectiveness and reliability of a biosensor start with the quality of the biorecognition step. A highly specific and sensitive biorecognition element ensures that the subsequent transduction step can accurately convert the interaction into a meaningful signal. For WGBs, the bio-recognition element typically involves enzymes, such as β-D-glucose oxidase (GOx) which catalyzes the oxidation of glucose, producing a detectable signal [[33]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0033), [[34]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0034), [[35]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0035).

WGB has the ability to provide continuous, real-time physiological data by dynamically measuring glucose levels in biofluids like sweat and tears [[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0013), [[36]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0036), [[37]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0037). Sweat has been the most readily accessible source of glucose, with the most sampling locations outside the body and ease of assortment device implantation [[38]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0038), [[39]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0039). Sweat glucose and blood glucose are also clearly linked, making it perfect for wearable glucose monitoring [[40]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0040), [[41]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0041), [[42]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0042). However, major obstacles remain in acquiring accurate sweat glucose data, notably the person-to-person variations due to environmental factors such as changes in pH and temperature, skin contamination [[43]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0043), [[44]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0044), irregular sampling without iontophoretic stimulation, a low generation rate, and the mixing of old and new samples [[45]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0045), [[46]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0046). More challenging is the low concentration of glucose found in sweat, which is estimated to be 100 times lower than in blood, necessitating the need for highly sensitive devices [[47]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0047), [[48]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0048), [[49]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0049).

Enzyme-based glucose sensing has emerged as a promising technology in wearable glucose monitoring, offering several benefits in addition to its specificity [[50]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0050), [[51]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0051) and the need for a small sample volume [[52]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0052), such as rapid response times, robustness, and cost-effectiveness [[53]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0053), [[54]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0054). The enzyme (GOx) has an inherent capacity to withstand some alterations in environmental factors that include pH, ionic strength, and temperature [[55]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0055), [[56]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0056), [[57]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0057), [[58]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0058), as well as patient use conditions essential for reliability and clinical application of continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) [[59]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0059), [[60]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0060), [[61]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0061). However, its restricted specificity to the β-anomer of D-glucose may limit its application in WGB, particularly sweat glucose monitoring [[62]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0062), [[63]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0063), [[64]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0064), [[65]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0065).

Metabolomic investigations indicate that α- and β-D-glucose anomers are present in biological samples at a relative percentage ratio estimated to be around 36-38 % and 62-64 % respectively [[66]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0066), [[67]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0067), [[68]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0068), [[69]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0069), [[70]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0070), [[71]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0071), [[72]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0072), [[73]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0073). The equilibrium distribution of the anomers remains constant under steady physiological conditions [[74]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0074), [[75]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0075) but can be altered by slight fluctuations in pH, temperature, or electrolytes [[76]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0076), [[77]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0077), [[78]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0078), [[79]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0079). When glucose samples are subjected to the GOx reaction, only the β-D-glucose anomer is oxidized [[80]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0080), [[81]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0081), [[82]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0082), [[83]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0083). The α-D-glucose anomer needs to be transformed into the β-anomer prior to oxidation [[84]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0084). Although this interconversion process may occur spontaneously, it generally requires time [[65]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0065), [[85]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0085), which might result in a longer response time or an underestimation of total glucose due to the incomplete conversion of α-D-glucose [[62]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0062), [[86]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0086), [[87]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0087). Blood glucose levels can be reliably estimated using the β-anomer due to constant physiological parameters [[88]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0088). However, alternative body fluids such as sweat may have variations in the D-glucose anomeric proportion due to external differences in electrolyte composition, fluid pH, and temperature, which can affect the equilibrium distributions and cause accuracy concerns [[62]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0062), [[77]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0077), [[89]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0089), [[90]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0090). This has prompted the need for a method to either maintain the anomeric equilibrium or entirely oxidize the total D-glucose anomers.

To accurately monitor glucose levels, β-D-glucose must be converted quickly and completely, as α-D-glucose cannot be oxidized by GOx [[91]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0091). MUT has been shown to improve glucose measurement accuracy by ensuring complete glucose detection [[92]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0092). Nakashima et al., [[93]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0093) designed the peroxyoxalate method for measuring glucose in human serum, which used MUT to speed up the response time. Guemas et al., [[85]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0085) developed a GOx&HRP&Ferrocene electrode for amperometric detection of sucrose and glucose, demonstrating the effect of pre-addition of invertase and MUT to the sample solution, resulting in a response time of only 2 min. The majority of the MUT-based investigations were aimed at enhancing the accuracy, sensitivity, and response time of glucose biosensors [[89]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0089), [[91]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0091), [[93]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0093), [[94]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0094), [[95]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0095), [[96]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266605392400047X#bib0096). Despite the enzyme’s promising potential, it has received less attention in recent research. This could be attributed to a lack of comprehensive research that investigates the enzyme’s complete range of functions while also recognizing potential obstacles.

Updating the present level of research on the use of MUT in glucose sensors is critical for furthering the development of wearable glucose biosensors (WGB) designed for alternate bodily fluids such as sweat. As a result, this review comprehensively assesses MUT’s potential for improving the sensitivity and accuracy of WGBs through signal amplification and accurate glucose detection, shedding light on its usefulness as a diabetes management tool. To the best of our knowledge, this kind of work is not available in existing literature. Therefore, by combining existing knowledge and identifying gaps in the literature, this study helps to shape the future trajectory of research in this vital area of healthcare technology.

Abstract
--------

Wearable biosensors are garnering substantial interest due to their potential to provide continuous, real-time physiological information in an array of healthcare-related applications via dynamic non-invasive measurements of chemical markers in biofluids, such as sweat, tears, saliva and interstitial fluid (ISF). Recent developments in this area have focused on electrochemical and optical biosensors, with major advances being made in the non-invasive monitoring of new biomarkers, ranging from metabolites to bacteria and hormones. These include the design of multiplexed biosensing approaches and microfluidic sampling/ transport systems, along with system integration and miniaturization combined with flexible materials for enhanced wearability and ease of operation. The increased accuracy, effectiveness and utility of modern wearable biosensing platforms are enhancing both reliability and commercial impact. Even so, our limited understanding of the correlations between analyte concentrations in the blood and non-invasive biofluids remains a major obstacle. In addition, both an expanded set of on-body bioaffinity bioassays and additional sensing strategies will be required to expand the scope and type of biomarkers accessible to monitoring. Further improvements in biosensor accuracy and stability in uncontrolled conditions, along with reproducible sample transport, will be required for improved sensor reliability. Overall, widespread acceptance of wearable biosensors by the medical and commercial communities will require extensive large population validation of their performance through multidisciplinary collaboration between the engineering, biological and clinical disciplines. Overall, real-time body sensing and communication of comprehensive physiological information via wearable biosensing technologies offer significant promise to enhance personal healthcare and performance monitoring with the potential to have a broad impact on our daily lives.

* * *

Wearable sensors have received tremendous attention since the arrival of smartphones and other mobile devices, owing to their ability to provide useful insights into the performance and health of individuals[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8183422/#R1)-[6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8183422/#R6). Early efforts in this area focused on physical sensors that monitored mobility and vital signs, such as steps, burned calories, or heart rate. The face of wearable devices has changed rapidly in recent years with researchers branching out from tracking physical exercise activity to focus on tackling major challenges in healthcare applications, such as the management of diabetes or remote monitoring of the elderly. To accomplish these goals, researchers have devoted substantial efforts to the development of wearable biosensors, which are defined as wearable sensing devices that incorporate a biological recognition element into the sensor operation (e.g., enzyme, antibody, cell receptor or organelle). The potential utility of wearable biosensors is evident from the rapidly increasing rate of newly reported proof-of-concept studies. Although several of these platforms are currently under clinical evaluation, successful translation to the commercial market has been lacking. Significant endeavors are currently underway toward the commercialization of non-invasive biosensors. However, these products still require further large-scale validation studies, the necessary regulatory approvals device and final marketing paths. Driven by the promise of the huge glucose sensing market, this commercial activity focuses largely on minimally-invasive glucose monitoring devices, as illustrated in the representative examples given in [Table 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8183422/#T1).

A typical biosensor contains two basic functional units: a ‘bioreceptor’ (e.g., enzyme, antibody or DNA) responsible for selective recognition of the target analyte, and a physico-chemical transducer (e.g., electrochemical, optical or mechanical) that translates this biorecognition event into a useful signal ([Fig. 1a](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8183422/#F1)). Such devices were initially developed for _in vitro_ measurements in controlled (laboratory or point-of-care) settings or for single-use home testing (e.g., blood glucose test strips). A brief history of biosensing technologies preceding current wearable biosensors is provided in [Fig. 1b](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8183422/#F1)[7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8183422/#R7)-[38](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8183422/#R38). These past advances have paved the way to modern wearable biosensors for non-invasive biomonitoring applications as an alternative to blood monitoring biomedical devices, in connection to wide range of healthcare applications.

Biosensor components and the path of biosensor development for wearables. **(a)** Schematic representation of biosensor operation principles: Target analyte detection by corresponding receptor molecule followed by signal transduction method and output. **(b)** The concept of enzyme electrodes was proposed by Clark and Lyons in 1962[7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8183422/#R7). Their device relied on entrapment of the enzyme glucose oxidase (GOx) over an amperometric oxygen electrode that monitored the oxygen consumed by the biocatalytic reaction. Clark’s electrochemical biosensor technology was transferred to the Yellow Spring Instrument (YSI) Company, which launched the first dedicated blood glucose analyzer (YSI Model 23 Analyzer) in 1975. Biosensors became a ‘hot’ topic during the 1980s, reflecting the growing emphasis on biotech. New biosensor transduction principles were introduced during this decade, including fiber-optic and mass-sensitive (piezoelectric) devices[8](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8183422/#R8)-[14](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8183422/#R14). Considerable efforts during the 1980s led also to the introduction of commercial self-testing blood glucose strips that used mediator-based enzyme electrodes[15](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8183422/#R15),[16](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8183422/#R16). Subsequent activity during the 1990s resulted in subcutaneously implantable needle-type electrodes for real-time in vivo glucose monitoring[17](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8183422/#R17). These subcutaneously implantable glucose sensors moved in the early 2000 to commercial continuous glucose monitors (CGMs) that track in real-time the glucose level in the ISF, along with diabetes relevant trends and patterns[18](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8183422/#R18),[19](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8183422/#R19). The emergence of nanotechnology in the late 1990s has led to variety of nanomaterial-based biosensors exploiting the attractive properties of different nanomaterials, such as silicon nanowires and gold nanoparticles, for label-free or amplified biosensing, respectively[20](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8183422/#R20),[21](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8183422/#R21). The specific base-pair recognition of DNA sequences led to the development of different DNA biosensors in the late 1990s[22](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8183422/#R22)-[24](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8183422/#R24). Such nucleic acid sensors are currently playing a growing role in genomic sequence analysis. These advances in biosensor technology over the past five decades paved the way to modern wearable biosensors, discussed in this article. Biosensors hold considerable promise for wearable applications due to their high specificity, speed, portability, low cost and low power requirements. Indeed, innovative biosensor platforms for non-invasive chemical analysis of biofluids, such as sweat, tears, saliva or interstitial fluid (ISF), have already been widely applied to a variety of head-to-toe, on-body application sites, targeting an array of important analytes in proof-of-concept demonstrations ([Fig. 2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8183422/#F2))[32](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8183422/#R32)-[36](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8183422/#R36),[39](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8183422/#R39)-[44](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8183422/#R44). Sweat, tears, saliva and ISF have been targeted as they can be sampled in a non-invasive manner, meaning that they can be readily accessed without disrupting the outermost protecting layers of the body’s skin (i.e., stratum corneum) and without contacting blood. As such, non-invasive sensing methods pose minimal risk of harm or infection and are generally more user-friendly.

The wide acceptance of such wearable biosensor technology requires a deep understanding of the biochemical composition of bodily fluids, such as sweat or tears, and its relation to blood chemistry. Wearable monitoring platforms can shed useful insights into dynamic biochemical processes in these biofluids by enabling continuous, real-time monitoring of biomarkers, which can be related to a wearer’s health and performance. Such real-time monitoring can provide information on wellness and health, enhance the management of chronic diseases and alert the user or medical professionals of abnormal or unforeseen situations. Wearable biosensors can obviate painful and risky blood sampling procedures and can be readily blended with a wearer’s daily routine. To accomplish this capability, the biosensing platform must provide direct contact with the sampled biofluids without inducing any discomfort to the wearer. Such body compliance can be achieved through use of advanced materials and smart designs that provide the necessary flexibility and stretchability[45](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8183422/#R45)-[48](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8183422/#R48). Continuous multidisciplinary development of new biosensing technologies (and corresponding new materials and energy sources) has led to numerous proof-of concept demonstrations and has driven growing efforts towards the commercialization activity of wearable sensors.

The attractive capabilities of modern wearable chemical and physical sensors and related research advances have been highlighted in several recent reviews[2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8183422/#R2), [29](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8183422/#R29), [48](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8183422/#R48)-[53](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8183422/#R53). Unlike physical or chemical wearable sensors, the wearable biosensors reviewed here rely on highly specific bioreceptors capable of recognizing target analytes in complex samples at their physiologically-relevant concentrations. Despite rapid progress in wearable biosensor technology over the past 5 years, we are only at the beginning of understanding how wearable biosensor technologies can improve health and performance.

In the following Review, we provide an overview of the key advances in wearable biosensors from the past 2 years and discuss their potential as alternatives to invasive biomedical devices and to gold standard blood assays. In particular, we discuss how the fundamental principles of biosensor systems can be adapted to the design of reliable wearable biosensors, we highlight key challenges in operating biosensors in specific non-invasive biofluids and the physiological relevance of monitoring key biomarkers in these fluids, and finally we provide an overview of the overall importance and future prospects of wearable biosensing devices for the biomedical field. Pioneering studies that greatly impacted the field of wearable biosensing are critically reviewed along with future challenges to overcome. The majority of the studies discussed here involve biosensing devices based on electrochemical signal transduction along with some optical-sensing devices, as these transduction mechanisms have been the most commonly reported on over the past several years. Emphasis is given to systems aiming at practical healthcare applications with promise for clinical translation in the near future.

The commercialization of wearable bioanalyte sensors is substantially more challenging than that of activity-tracking counterparts or common lab-based biosensors because such devices must be capable not only of continuous on-body biochemical sensing but also of reliable measurement of a biorecognition element (or elements) that is highly specific yet fragile. Robust, reliable measurement also must overcome such challenges as gradual surface biofouling at the body–sensor interface, inefficient transport of sample over the sensor, limited stability of many bioreceptors, the complexity of multi-step bioaffinity assays and related receptor regeneration, and issues posed by calibration for on-body biosensors. In each section below, we discuss specific challenges related to each particular system and biofluid. Finally, we discuss future research and commercialization prospects, existing bottlenecks and present our perspective on the prospects for this exciting research area.

Epidermal wearable biosensors
-----------------------------

Taking into account that the epidermis covers an overwhelming majority of our body, skin-worn conformal devices have received the greatest recent attention among the various types of wearable biosensors. Epidermal biosensors can facilitate real-time analysis of biomarkers in epidermal biofluids (sweat and ISF) with some systems exhibiting continuous monitoring capabilities toward a variety of biomedical and fitness applications. These devices rely on sweat or ISF sampling at the skin surface, along with transport of these biofluids over the biosensor surface. Such skin-worn biosensors commonly rely on different transduction modes (e.g., optical, electrochemical, and mechanical) along with biocatalytic and ion-recognition receptors. Further integration with data processing and transmission components are necessary for a fully wearable platform. The majority of recent reports, however, have focused on electrochemical and colorimetric transduction methodologies. Major progress has been made toward a variety of skin-worn platforms offering the capability to readily sample epidermal biofluids along with wearer comfort[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8183422/#R3), [34](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8183422/#R34), [35](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8183422/#R35), [40](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8183422/#R40), [42](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8183422/#R42), [50](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8183422/#R50), [54](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8183422/#R54)-[63](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8183422/#R63). Such devices have been realized through direct transfer of sensors onto the skin (using E-skin or printed temporary tattoos), by sensor incorporation into wrist-bands and patches, or by embedding sensors directly into textiles to ensure tight contact with the skin while allowing endurance of mechanical stresses encountered during body movements.

### Secretion and composition of epidermal biofluids (sweat, ISF).

Sweat is the most readily obtainable biofluid for chemical sensing applications since sweat glands are distributed across the entire body with more than 100 glands/cm 2 of skin. This physiology provides the most viable sampling sites and surface area outside the body. However, sweat must be excreted to the outer skin surface to be analyzed. Such sweat generation can be accomplished through exercise activity, thermal heating, stress, or iontophoretic stimulation. Generally, sweat contains metabolites (e.g., lactate, glucose, urea, ethanol or cortisol) along with electrolytes (e.g., sodium, potassium, chloride or ammonium), trace elements (e.g., zinc or copper), and small amounts of large molecules (e.g., proteins, nucleic acids, neuropeptides or cytokines)[64](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8183422/#R64). These biomarkers make _in situ_ sweat analysis of considerable interest for non-invasive monitoring of physiological health status (e.g., hydration or physical stress) and for disease diagnosis and management (e.g., in such conditions as cystic fibrosis or diabetes). Non-invasive monitoring at the epidermis eliminates issues related with blood sampling while maintaining the protective stratum corneum skin layer intact. Yet, additional research is needed for determining and validating the clinical value of sweat as a diagnostic biofluid. Target sweat analytes are each transported to the sweat from surrounding capillaries with unique partitioning profiles, making reliable correlation to concurrent blood concentrations difficult. Analytes can reach the sweat by passive (i.e., diffusion) or active mechanisms and can be also generated within the sweat duct itself. Although variations in sweat rate can be monitored using multiplexed analysis (i.e., simultaneous monitoring of analytes with concentration profiles that are independent of sweat rate) or skin impedance measurements, the degree of analyte dilution during sweat excretion is affected by the relationship between sweat rate and analyte partitioning rate[64](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8183422/#R64). Deeper understandings of sweat chemistry and transport, along with advances in sweat sampling and detection technologies, should accelerate sweat-based diagnostic opportunities.

### Subjects

## Abstract

Wearable health sensors could monitor the wearer's health and surrounding environment in real-time. With the development of sensor and operating system hardware technology, the functions of wearable devices have been gradually enriched with more diversified forms and more accurate physiological indicators. These sensors are moving towards high precision, continuity, and comfort, making great contributions to improving personalized health care. At the same time, in the context of the rapid development of the Internet of Things, the ubiquitous regulatory capabilities have been released. Some sensor chips are equipped with data readout and signal conditioning circuits, and a wireless communication module for transmitting data to computer equipment. At the same time, for data analysis of wearable health sensors, most companies use artificial neural networks (ANN). In addition, artificial neural networks could help users effectively get relevant health feedback. Through the physiological response of the human body, various sensors worn could effectively transmit data to the control unit, which analyzes the data and provides feedback of the health value to the user through the computer. This is the working principle of wearable sensors for health. This article focuses on wearable biosensors used for healthcare monitoring in different situations, as well as the development, technology, business, ethics, and future of wearable sensors for health monitoring.

ABSTRACT
--------

Wearable biosensor systems represent a significant breakthrough in the life sciences, offering real-time monitoring and quantitative assessment of various human health parameters. The escalating demand for continuous and immediate surveillance of both acute and chronic conditions, compounded by constraints in clinical infrastructure, has propelled extensive investigation into wearable biosensors. These systems have emerged as pivotal tools for furnishing physiological insights and facilitating non-invasive monitoring. They monitor various analytes through the analysis of sweat, epidermal, Interstitial fluids, breath and tears, and easily incorporated into flexible substrates ensuring long-term wear and continuous monitoring. However, these biosensors penetrate deeper into the body's fluid networks, providing a systemic approach to continuous monitoring of biomarkers, which is particularly useful for individuals with metabolic disorders. This comprehensive review explores recent trends and diverse applications of wearable biosensors. These applications empower individuals to dynamically assess their well-being, enabling informed healthcare decisions tailored to individual needs. The convergence of these approaches in wearable biosensors highlights a comprehensive approach to understand individual health. This integration holds the promise of transforming healthcare practice by providing continuous observance and remote monitoring. The result has been a paradigm shift towards active, personalized health care, leading to improved health outcomes and improved quality of life

1. Introduction
---------------

The increasing focus on biosensor advancement is attracting significant interest in biomedicine and healthcare sectors, primarily because of their extensive application in clinical practice, pharmacy, and routine healthcare monitoring [[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0001),[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0002)]. A biosensor is a combined receptor-transducer analytical device designed to detect alterations in biological processes and convert them into electrical signals[2]. They encompass a biological sensing component (enzymes, nucleic acids, proteins, etc.) and a physicochemical transducer that converts chemical reactions into discernible electrical signals correlating with the concentration of the analyte [[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0003)] as shown in [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#fig0001).

Fig. 1. Schematic Representation of Conventional Biosensors comprising of distinct Bioreceptors and different type of Transducers**.**

The advantages of biosensors include their cost-effectiveness, compact size, swift and straightforward operation, as well as superior selectivity and sensitivity. One notable example is the glucose oxidase biosensor utilized by individuals with diabetes to monitor blood sugar levels [[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0004)]. However, the routine monitoring of blood sugar levels depends significantly on in-person appointments, manual examinations, and treatment methods that involve substantial human interaction [[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0005),[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0006)]. Also, these biosensors are often crafted from rigid materials, and rely on invasive blood extraction at specific intervals limiting their comfort and suitability for extended wear [[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0007)]. In contrast, the emergence of wearable biosensors enables the detection and monitoring of biomarkers and therapeutic substances from bodily fluids (tears, sweat, interstitial fluid, and saliva) in a minimally invasive or noninvasive and continuous manner [[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0008)]. They are compact electronic gadgets that incorporate sensors either onto or within the human body, taking forms such as eyeglasses, smart watches, tattoos, gloves, clothing, or implants facilitating in vivo sensing, data recording, and computation using mobile or portable devices [[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0009),[10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0010)]. They offer vital insights into the wearer's current condition and are poised to become a compelling option due to their notable attributes such as affordability, portability, sensitivity, timely diagnosis, and management of medical conditions, especially for managing chronic diseases [[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0011)]. Wearable technologies have undergone progressive development, combining multiplexed biosensing, microfluidic sampling, and transport systems with flexible materials and body attachments to enhance wearability and user convenience. In the evolution of wearable biosensors, the incorporation of sensors to detect a wide range of biomarkers poses a significant challenge, necessitating continuous advancements in sensing technology. Electrochemical-based sensors stand out among various types of biosensors due to their advantages in ease of construction, heightened sensitivity, rapid response, and low power consumption. The constitution of wearable sensors heavily relies on sensing electrodes, predominantly employing the electrochemical approach. However, challenges persist regarding critical aspects like functional materials and manufacturing techniques employed in the fabrication of sensing electrodes, underscoring the need for improvement to enhance the performance of wearable biochemical sensors [[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0009),[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0012)]. Currently, Internet of Things (IoT) is a highly intriguing subject that offers solutions to overcome these constraints in multiple ways. Certain features, notably the inclusion of wireless power and data transmission capabilities, have markedly heightened the demand for wearable biosensors in the context of remote monitoring and Internet of Things (IoT)-driven sensing applications. Wearable devices, as part of the Internet of Things (IoT), have been developed to assist individuals in receiving appropriate medical care. These smart devices are connected to the internet to collect and transmit data, as well as receive information to facilitate informed decisions, evolving from simple accessories to more specialized and practical utilities. Additionally, these smart wearables can interface with other device groups, such as smartphones, for communication and computational purposes [[[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0013), [[14]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0014), [[15]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0015)]. Furthermore, these platforms exhibit outstanding attributes, including exceptional flexibility and stretchability, coupled with remarkable sensitivity, enabling them to discern a broad spectrum of signals with precision. However, further studies, including cohort validation and performance evaluation, are necessary to establish the clinical reliability and acceptance of wearable biosensors. In recent times, considerable attention has been directed towards developing wearable sensors, reflecting a growing focus on identifying diverse biomarkers that influence health. With advancements in miniaturized devices and technologies such as microelectronics and wireless communication, wearable biochemical sensors have become deeply integrated into our daily lives. Nevertheless, further development is necessary for the future [[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0016),[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0017)].

This comprehensive review critically explores the adaptation of wearable sensors for detecting a variety of analytes, including body fluids. The paper highlights the application of wearable biosensors in clinical practice and healthcare monitoring. It delves into previously conducted research, elucidating the underlying working principles, fabrication methodologies, and the utilization of flexible electrochemical biosensors incorporating nanoparticles and bio receptors for precise analyte detection. It explores the principles underlying biosensors, highlighting their role in detecting and converting biological processes into electrical signals. The paper discusses the advantages of biosensors, such as cost-effectiveness and superior sensitivity, as well as their limitations, including invasiveness and rigidity. A key novelty of this review lies in its systematic classification of wearable biosensing technologies based on the specific biological secretions, namely tears, sweat, epidermal fluids, interstitial fluid (ISF), and exhaled breath. By adopting this secretion-specific framework, the review provides a unique perspective on the design considerations, analytical performance, and application-specific challenges associated with each fluid type. Moreover, it highlights the emergence of next-generation wearable biosensors capable of enabling continuous, non-invasive monitoring, thereby paving the way for decentralized and proactive healthcare models. The review concludes by outlining future research directions, advocating for the development of biocompatible materials, miniaturized power sources, and multi-analyte detection platforms. By addressing both current capabilities and unmet needs, this work provides a valuable roadmap for advancing wearable biosensor technology toward robust, market-ready solutions.

2. Wearable Biosensors: a holistic approach to monitoring health
----------------------------------------------------------------

With advancements in sensor and operating system hardware technology, wearable devices have seen a gradual expansion of functionality and an improvement in the accuracy of physiological indicators. These sensors are now characterized by their increased precision, continuous monitoring capabilities, and enhanced comfort, making significant contributions to personalized healthcare [[18](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0018)]. Their primary purpose is to assist users in monitoring their own health and providing timely alerts to those in need. Wearable sensors offer the distinct advantage of enabling users to engage in scientific health monitoring at any time and in any location. This eliminates the need for frequent clinic visits and allows users to save valuable time by wearing portable sensors.

The benefits of wearable sensors are particularly notable for users who make use of a variety of portable sensors. For individuals with chronic illnesses, wearable devices have the potential to reduce the frequency of hospitalizations. By allowing users to adjust their daily routines and diets based on device data, these devices can help in timely health management, ultimately leading to cost savings on expensive treatments. Wearable devices can also replace traditional medical equipment like ECG gadgets and defibrillators by providing precise measurements of body temperature and heart health, resulting in potential cost savings for hospitals [[19](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0019)].

Additionally, wearable devices empower patients to take better control of their lives and health. They offer real-time insights into an individual's current physical condition, which can be invaluable in ensuring timely hospitalization. As people increasingly prioritize their health with improving living standards, there has been a surge of interest in heart rate [[20](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0020)], blood oxygen monitoring [[21](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0021),[22](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0022)], sports and fitness, and related equipment [[23](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0023)].

In recent years, the landscape of wearable technology has undergone a significant transformation. Researchers have shifted their focus from tracking regular exercise routines to addressing pressing healthcare challenges, such as diabetic patient management and geriatric remote monitoring [[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0016)]. To tackle these complex issues, scientists have concentrated on the development of wearable biosensors, which incorporate biometric elements into sensor operations, including enzymes, antibodies, cell receptors, or organelles [[18](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0018)]. Various biological fluids found in the human body, including tears, sweat, interstitial fluid (ISF), epidermal fluids, and breath, serve as valuable sources for developing devices that enable early disease detection [[[24]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0024), [[25]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0025), [[26]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0026), [[27]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0027), [[28]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0028)]. [Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#tbl0001) compiles various biological fluid used for the development of wearable biosensors along with their advantages and limitations.

Table 1. Comparison of Various Biological Fluids for Wearable Biosensing Applications.

| Biological Fluid | Key Source | Accessibility | Biomarker Accuracy | Analytical Complexity | Advantages | Limitations |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Tear** | Lachrymal Glands | Moderate (via eye-wearable devices) | High – due to direct diffusion of analytes from blood | Low – streamlined detection of proteins, electrolytes, and metabolites | High precision and specificity, similar composition to blood | Limited volume, collection can be challenging without irritation |
| **Sweat** | Eccrine and apocrine sweat glands (via skin) | High – readily available across the body surface | Moderate – concentrations can vary based on activity and hydration | Moderate – influenced by sweat rate, evaporation, and contamination | Non-invasive, widely accessible, suitable for continuous monitoring | Biomarker concentration variability, potential environmental interferences |
| **ISF** | Between skin cells and capillaries | Moderate – accessed via microneedles or skin-permeable patches | High – closely reflects blood analyte levels | Moderate to High – requires minimally invasive access tools | High biomarker relevance; stable composition | Requires specialized access methods; slower sample replenishment |
| **Epidermal** | Outer skin layers | Low – small quantities | Low to Moderate | High – complex matrix and low volume | Potential for superficial, non-invasive sampling | Low fluid volume; difficult to isolate specific analytes |
| **Breath** | Lungs (exhaled air) | High – easy collection | Low to Moderate – depends on target gases (e.g., VOCs) | High – complex and variable due to environmental and physiological factors | Non-invasive; continuous sampling possible | Variability due to diet, environment, and metabolism |

Among the various types of wearable biosensors, skin-worn biosensor devices have garnered significant attention, primarily because the epidermis covers a substantial portion of the human body [[29](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0029)]. These devices rely on collecting perspiration from the skin's surface or sampling interstitial fluid, facilitating the flow of biofluids across the biosensor surface. The widespread distribution of sweat glands across the body makes perspiration the most readily accessible biofluid for chemical sensing applications. As a result, these biosensor devices offer a promising avenue for efficient and non-invasive disease monitoring [[30](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0030),[31](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0031)]. These sensors can be attached to the body through various means, including standard clothing, watches, mobile phones, and other everyday items integrated with sensors. Flexible sensors have emerged as a promising innovation, with advances in printed electronics and materials enabling tiny sensors to be used as skin patches. These biosensors are capable of measuring skin conductance, heart rate, body temperature, and detecting changes in pH [[32](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0032),[33](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0033)], glucose [[34](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0034)], and salt levels [[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0016),[35](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0035),[36](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0036)] within the human body. enriched_points.expanded_content: Wearable biosensors must ensure high accuracy in real-time biomarker detection. Variabilities in environmental conditions affect performance, necessitating advanced materials and methods for reliable readings.
User experience is critical for wearable biosensors, focusing on comfort, ease of use, and seamless integration into daily life. Ergonomic designs enhance user compliance and long-term adoption.

Protecting user data is paramount; wearable biosensors must incorporate robust security measures to prevent unauthorized access and ensure compliance with data protection regulations, fostering user trust.

Cost-effectiveness is vital for widespread adoption of wearable biosensors. Efficient manufacturing processes and competitive pricing strategies can enhance accessibility while maintaining performance standards.
